summary: Gruber dismantles Cory Doctorow's alarm over Apple's use of a TPM chip in the Intel Developer Transition Kit. The TPM is being used solely to prevent Mac OS X from running on non-Apple hardware — exactly what Apple publicly stated it would do. Doctorow extrapolated this into a dystopian scenario where applications would use TPM to lock users out of their own data, but Gruber argues there is zero evidence Apple intends anything of the sort. He points out that both Windows Vista and the Linux kernel also include TPM support, undermining the idea that its mere presence is sinister. Gruber compares the unfounded hysteria to McCarthyism and criticizes Tim O'Reilly for amplifying it uncritically.
key_points: Apple's TPM usage in the Developer Transition Kit serves a single purpose: tying Mac OS X to Apple hardware, exactly as Apple publicly announced.
Doctorow's scenario — where TPM enables apps to lock users out of their own files — is entirely fabricated with no supporting evidence.
Both Windows Vista and the Linux kernel include TPM support, making it absurd to single out Apple as uniquely threatening.
Good intentions don't excuse spreading unfounded fear — influential voices carry responsibility for accuracy.
Open file formats matter more than free software, and Apple's TPM usage does nothing to threaten them.
money_quotes: There is no indication, none, zero, not even a whiff, that Apple intends to enable, let alone encourage, developers to create software with the TPM file-access authorization-locking described by Doctorow above. None.
This is not about third-party software developers limiting access to your data. This is about Apple limiting access to their operating system.
There is a word for these people. That word is asshole. No, wait, zealot. OK, there are two words for these people.

The idea that Doctorow's critique, despite being unfounded, was good for the community simply because of its anti-DRM slant is like saying McCarthyism was good for the country simply because of its anti-Communist slant.
If a file is written to disk in an open format, no TPM chip can prevent you from opening it in another program.
TPM support, in and of itself, is no more or less evil than support for any other chips on a motherboard.
The only 'proposed DRM' that would get in the way of any 'freedom to switch' exists only in completely fabricated scenarios from Doctorow himself.
key_insight: The mere presence of a TPM chip used as a hardware dongle for OS licensing is fundamentally different from using TPM to restrict users' access to their own data, and conflating the two is irresponsible fearmongering.
